cover.title: Git Basics: Why Every Security Pro __Needs Git__
cover.hook: Every script, rule, config, and policy you write needs version control. Git week starts now.

slides.label: What is Git?
slides.method: WHAT — Git Explained for Security Professionals
slides.heading: Git: __Version Control__ for Security Work

body.text: **Git** is a distributed version control system that tracks every change ever made to every file in a project. Every security script, detection rule, incident playbook, hardening configuration, and policy document should live in Git.

**Why security professionals need Git:** It creates an auditable history of every change — who made it, when, and why. This is non-repudiation applied to your own work. Every professional security environment uses Git without exception.

**Git is not optional.** In 2026, submitting scripts or tools without Git history is the equivalent of submitting work with no version history, no collaboration capability, and no audit trail. It signals junior-level thinking regardless of technical skill.

slides.time: 20 min · git-scm.com/book — free Pro Git book

slides.label: Core Git Concepts
slides.heading: Git Vocabulary: __6 Concepts to Know Cold__

body.text: **Repository (repo):** The project folder with complete change history. Contains all versions of every file since the project began. Your entire project's memory.
**Commit:** A named snapshot of your project at a specific moment. Includes a message describing what changed and why. The fundamental unit of Git history.
**Branch:** A parallel version of the codebase. Create a branch to develop a new scanner feature or detection rule without touching the stable main version.
**Merge:** Combine two branches back together. Integrates completed work into the main codebase after review.
**Remote:** A hosted copy of the repository (GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket). Where your team collaborates and where your portfolio lives publicly.
**Clone:** Your local copy of a remote repository. What you get when you `git clone` someone else's project.

slides.time: 20 min · try.github.io — GitHub's official 25-minute interactive tutorial

slides.label: First-Time Setup
slides.method: HOW — Configuring Git on a New Machine
slides.heading: First-Time Git __Setup__: One Machine, Once

body.text:
# Run these once per machine — stored in ~/.gitconfig:
git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "you@email.com"
git config --global core.editor "nano"  # or 'vim' or 'code'

# Verify your configuration:
git config --list
# Output:
# user.name=Your Name
# user.email=you@email.com
# core.editor=nano

# Generate SSH key for GitHub auth (recommended over HTTPS):
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "you@email.com"
cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub  # Copy this to GitHub Settings → SSH Keys
**Skipping setup creates problems:** Without name/email config, your commits have no author attribution — making git blame and audit trails useless in team environments.

slides.time: 15 min · Set up Git on your machine right now

slides.label: The Daily Git Workflow
slides.method: HOW — The 4 Commands That Cover 90% of Git
slides.heading: Daily Workflow: __4 Commands__, Endless Power

body.text:
# 1. Create a new repository:
git init my-security-tools
cd my-security-tools

# 2. Stage changes for commit:
git add scanner.py          # Stage one file
git add .                   # Stage ALL changes
git status                  # See what is staged

# 3. Commit staged changes with a message:
git commit -m "Add threaded port scanner with banner grabbing"
# Message format: verb + what + brief why

# 4. Push to GitHub remote:
git remote add origin git@github.com:user/repo.git
git push -u origin main     # First push sets upstream
git push                    # Every push after that

slides.time: 25 min · git-scm.com/book Chapter 2 — Git Basics

slides.label: What to Learn Today
slides.heading: Day 57 Checklist
steps: **Install Git** and run `git config --global` setup — verify with `git config --list`
**Create a GitHub account** if you do not have one — set up SSH key authentication
**Create a repo** called `cybersecurity-tools` — push your Python port scanner from Day 30
**Write a README.md** — tool name, description, installation steps, usage example with output
Understand **why every security file needs Git** — audit trail, collaboration, non-repudiation
slides.time: Total Day 57 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes

resources.label: 📖 Book
resources.name: Pro Git Book — Free, Definitive Git Reference

slides.label: Action Plan
slides.heading: Your Hands-On __Task__ for Day 57
steps: **Create** a GitHub account → generate an SSH key → add the public key to GitHub Settings
**Create** a new public repo: `cybersecurity-tools` — initialise with a README
**Clone** the repo locally → add your port scanner from Day 30 → write a proper README.md
**Commit and push:** `git add .` → `git commit -m 'Add port scanner with banner grabbing'` → `git push`
slides.time: Hands-On Task: 60 – 75 minutes

slides.label: Pro Tip
slides.quote: Your GitHub profile IS your security portfolio. Hiring managers and recruiters check GitHub before interviews. Consistent commits over 90 days are visible proof of dedication that no cover letter can replicate.
slides.sub: Write commit messages that explain WHY a change was made, not just WHAT. 'Fix port scanner' is useless. 'Fix port scanner timeout causing false FILTERED results on firewalled hosts' tells a story. Good commit messages are searchable, reviewable, and demonstrate engineering maturity to every recruiter who reads your history.
slides.mistake: Committing API keys, passwords, or .env files. Create a `.gitignore` file BEFORE your very first commit on any project. GitHub secret scanning finds exposed keys within seconds of a push — and so do automated scrapers. Exposed keys get abused within minutes. Prevention is infinitely easier than rotation and damage control.

cover.title: Branching, Merging & __Pull Requests__
cover.hook: Branches let you experiment without breaking production. The safety net every team needs.

slides.label: Why Branches?
slides.method: WHAT — The Purpose of Git Branches
slides.heading: Branches: __Safe Experimentation__ at Scale

body.text: **A branch is a parallel, isolated copy of your codebase.** Changes on a branch do not affect any other branch until you explicitly merge them. This makes it safe to experiment, develop, and test without risk to working code.
**Security engineering use:** Test a new Suricata detection rule on a branch — if it causes false positives, delete the branch with zero impact on production rules.
**Development use:** Build a new scanner feature in isolation — commit frequently without breaking the working version that others depend on.
**Emergency use:** Apply a critical hotfix on a dedicated branch — merge it immediately without waiting for incomplete feature work to be ready.
**The fundamental rule:** `main` branch is always stable and deployable. Work happens on branches. This is universal across every professional engineering team.

slides.time: 20 min · learngitbranching.js.org — free visual interactive tutorial

slides.label: Branch Commands
slides.heading: Branch Commands: __Create, Switch, Delete__

body.text:
# Create a new branch AND switch to it:
git checkout -b feature-dns-scanner
# Equivalent to: git branch feature-dns-scanner && git checkout feature-dns-scanner

# List all local branches (* = current):
git branch
# List all branches including remote:
git branch -a

# Switch between existing branches:
git checkout main              # Back to main
git checkout feature-dns-scanner  # Back to feature

# Delete a branch after merging:
git branch -d feature-dns-scanner   # Safe delete
git branch -D feature-dns-scanner   # Force delete (even if unmerged)

# Push a new branch to GitHub:
git push -u origin feature-dns-scanner

slides.time: 20 min · learngitbranching.js.org — complete levels 1–4

slides.label: Merging
slides.method: HOW — Merging Branches and Resolving Conflicts
slides.heading: Merging: __Combining Branches__ Back Together

body.text:
# Merge feature branch into main:
git checkout main                     # Switch to target branch
git merge feature-dns-scanner         # Merge the feature in
git branch -d feature-dns-scanner     # Clean up after merge

# Two merge types:
# Fast-forward (clean, no divergence):
# main: A---B
# feature: A---B---C
# Result: A---B---C (linear)

# 3-way merge (branches diverged):
# main: A---B---D
# feature: A---B---C
# Result: A---B---D---E (merge commit)

**Merge conflicts** happen when the same line was edited differently in both branches. Git marks the conflict with `<<<<<<` and `>>>>>>>` markers. Open the file, choose the correct version, delete the markers, then `git add filename` + `git commit` to complete the merge.

slides.time: 25 min · Practice merging two branches with a deliberate conflict

slides.label: Pull Requests
slides.heading: Pull Requests: __Code Review Before Deployment__

body.text: **Create branch** — `git checkout -b feature-ioc-checker` — write your code and commit to the branch.
**Push branch** to GitHub — `git push -u origin feature-ioc-checker` — the branch now exists on the remote.
**Open Pull Request** on GitHub — navigate to the repo → 'Compare & pull request' → write a description of what changed and why → submit for review.
**Review & discuss** — teammates review the diff, leave comments, request changes. Discussion is preserved permanently in the PR thread — full audit trail.
**Merge** — once approved, merge the PR into main. In security teams, ALL changes to detection rules, scripts, and policies go through PR review. No exceptions.

slides.time: 20 min · Open a PR on your cybersecurity-tools repo

slides.label: What to Learn Today
slides.heading: Day 58 Checklist
steps: Create a branch **`feature-banner-grabber`** — add your Day 31 banner grabber script to it
**Push the branch** to GitHub — open a Pull Request describing what the tool does
**Review your own PR** — leave a comment, then merge it into main
Practice **resolving a merge conflict** — edit the same line in both branches, merge, and resolve
Complete **learngitbranching.js.org levels 1–6** — visual mastery of branch concepts
slides.time: Total Day 58 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes

slides.label: Action Plan
slides.heading: Your Hands-On __Task__ for Day 58
steps: **Create** branch `feature-banner-grabber` → add banner grabber script → commit with a descriptive message
**Push** the branch to GitHub → open a Pull Request with title and description
**Review** your own PR → add one comment → merge → delete the feature branch
**Practice** a conflict: edit README.md in both main and a test branch → merge → resolve conflict manually
slides.time: Hands-On Task: 60 – 75 minutes

slides.label: Pro Tip
slides.quote: In your first security job, every script, rule, and policy change will go through a Pull Request for peer review before it touches production. Learning this workflow now means Day 1 feels natural.
slides.sub: Git Flow branching strategy is the professional standard: `main` (always stable) → `develop` (integration) → `feature/*` (one branch per feature) → `hotfix/*` (emergency production fixes). When you use this naming convention in personal projects, it signals that you have worked in professional team environments — even if you have not yet.
slides.mistake: Never push directly to `main` in any team environment. Feature branches and PRs are non-negotiable professional practice. Even for solo projects, using branches and PRs trains the habit. The first time you push a breaking change directly to production main in a real job, you will understand why this rule exists.

cover.title: GitHub Portfolio & __Secret Scanning__
cover.hook: Your GitHub profile IS your security resume. Here is how to make it impossible to ignore.

slides.label: GitHub Profile Optimization
slides.method: WHAT — Building a Professional GitHub Profile
slides.heading: GitHub Profile: __Your Security Resume__

body.text: **GitHub profile = professional identity.** Security engineering hiring managers and technical recruiters check GitHub profiles before scheduling interviews. A well-crafted profile with documented projects communicates competence before you say a single word.
**Profile README:** Create a repository named EXACTLY the same as your username — GitHub automatically renders its README.md on your profile page. This is your professional headline.

**Pinned repositories:** Pin your 6 best projects. These are the first things visitors see. Choose projects that demonstrate breadth — scanner, log analyser, OSINT tool, incident report, Sigma rule, automation script.

**Contribution graph:** The green square grid. Consistent daily commits over 90 days creates a dense, impressive pattern. Employers see this as visible proof of sustained effort — something impossible to fake.
**Professional headline:** Target role + key skills. 'Security Engineer | Python | Wireshark | Suricata | CTF Player' tells the whole story in one line.

slides.time: 20 min · docs.github.com/en/account-and-profile

slides.label: Writing a Great README
slides.heading: README: The __First Impression__ of Every Repo

body.text: **Tool name + one-line description:** What does this tool do? Who is it for? Make it specific — 'Threaded port scanner with service banner grabbing and NVD CVE correlation' beats 'port scanner'.
**Features list:** 3–5 bullet points showing specific capabilities. Use action verbs: Scans, Extracts, Correlates, Generates, Alerts.
**Installation:** Exact commands from zero to running. `git clone`, `pip install -r requirements.txt`, done. Never assume the reader has anything pre-installed.
**Usage with real output:** Show the actual terminal output. Screenshots or ASCII blocks showing real results build immediate credibility.

**Ethical use disclaimer:** 'For authorised security testing only. Do not use against systems you do not own or have explicit permission to test.' This signals professional maturity and legal awareness.

slides.time: 25 min · Write or rewrite 3 READMEs using this structure

slides.label: GitHub Secret Scanning
slides.heading: Secret Scanning: __Find Exposed Keys__

body.text: **GitHub automatically scans every public push** for exposed secrets — AWS access keys, API tokens, private keys, database credentials. If found, GitHub notifies you and the service provider immediately.

# Enable secret scanning for private repos:
# GitHub repo → Settings → Security → Secret scanning
# → Enable secret scanning
# → Enable push protection (blocks pushes with secrets)
**Push Protection** (free for public repos) blocks the push entirely before the secret is exposed. This is the correct setting — prevention over detection.

**If a secret IS exposed:** Rotate it IMMEDIATELY — assume it was scraped by automated bots within 60 seconds of exposure. Changing the secret in your codebase and revoking the old one are both required. The exposed secret in git history must also be removed with `git filter-repo`.

slides.time: 15 min · Enable secret scanning on all your repos now

slides.label: Pre-commit Hooks & .gitignore
slides.heading: Pre-commit Hooks: __Block Secrets Before Commit__

body.text:
# Install detect-secrets (blocks secrets before commit):
pip install detect-secrets

# Scan your repo for existing secrets:
detect-secrets scan > .secrets.baseline
detect-secrets audit .secrets.baseline

# Essential .gitignore entries for security projects:
.env
*.pem
*.key
*.p12
config/secrets*
*credentials*
venv/
__pycache__/
.DS_Store
*.log
reports/
.secrets.baseline

# Generate complete .gitignore automatically:
# Visit gitignore.io → select Python, Linux, macOS, VS Code
# → copy the generated file to your project root
# ALWAYS create .gitignore BEFORE the very first commit
**Critical rule:** `.gitignore` only prevents FUTURE commits. If a secret was already committed, it lives in git history forever until you use `git filter-repo` to surgically remove it.

slides.time: 20 min · Set up .gitignore and detect-secrets on all projects

slides.label: What to Learn Today
slides.heading: Day 59 Checklist
steps: Create your **GitHub Profile README** — skills, current learning, top 3 projects, LinkedIn link
**Pin 6 repositories** on your profile — choose projects that show breadth of skills
Add **.gitignore** to every repo that does not have one — use gitignore.io to generate it
Enable **secret scanning and push protection** on all your repositories
Run `git log --all -p | grep -i 'password\|api_key\|secret'` — audit all repos for exposed secrets
slides.time: Total Day 59 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes

resources.label: 🏷️ Badges
resources.name: shields.io — Add Professional Badges to READMEs

slides.label: Action Plan
slides.heading: Your Hands-On __Task__ for Day 59
steps: **Create** your GitHub Profile README — repository named exactly the same as your username
**Audit** every repo: add .gitignore, add requirements.txt (Python repos), rewrite README with usage examples
**Run** the secret search: `git log --all -p | grep -i 'password\|api_key\|secret'` — fix any findings
**Enable** push protection on all repos: Settings → Security → Secret scanning → Push protection
slides.time: Hands-On Task: 75 – 90 minutes

slides.label: Pro Tip
slides.quote: If you find a secret in git history, deleting the file does not help — it is still in every commit. Use `git filter-repo` to surgically remove it, force-push, then rotate the credential immediately.
slides.sub: Add shields.io badges to every README — Python version badge, license badge, last-commit badge. They take 5 minutes per repo and immediately signal that the project is maintained and professional. Recruiters who see a repo with proper badges, a clear README, and a GitHub Actions status badge make an instant positive assessment before reading a single line of code.
slides.mistake: Running `git rm` to delete a file containing a secret and thinking the problem is solved. `git rm` removes the file from the working tree and stages the deletion — but the file still exists in every previous commit in the history. Anyone who clones the repo and runs `git log -p` can still see the secret. Use `git filter-repo` for real removal.

cover.title: Advanced Git: __log, bisect, stash & blame__
cover.hook: Senior engineers use these Git commands daily. Master them and stand out in every technical interview.

slides.label: git log — Audit Trail
slides.method: WHAT — git log for Forensic History
slides.heading: git log: __Complete Audit Trail__

body.text:
# Compact one-line history:
git log --oneline
# a3f2b1c Add DNS scanner with subdomain enumeration
# 8d4e7f2 Fix timeout causing false FILTERED results
# 2c1a9b8 Initial port scanner — TCP threaded version

# Visual branch tree:
git log --graph --all --oneline

# Every change ever made to one file:
git log -p scanner.py

# Filter by author and date:
git log --since='1 week ago' --author='Alice'
git log --since='2026-01-01' --until='2026-04-01'

**Security forensic use:** In incident response on a compromised codebase, `git log -p filename` shows every change ever made to a specific file — including who made it, when, and exactly what bytes changed. This is your first step when investigating suspicious code changes.

slides.time: 20 min · git-scm.com/book Chapter 2 · ohshitgit.com

slides.label: git blame
slides.method: WHAT — Who Changed What and When
slides.heading: git blame: __Who Changed This Line?__

body.text:
# Show who last changed every line of a file:
git blame firewall_rules.py

# Output format:
# commit_hash (Author Date Time Timezone LineNum) Code
# a3f2b1c (Alice 2026-03-15 14:22:01 +0000   1) #!/usr/bin/env python3
# 8d4e7f2 (Bob   2026-04-01 09:15:43 +0000   2) import socket

# Blame a specific range of lines:
git blame -L 10,25 scanner.py

# Blame with commit context:
git blame -e scanner.py  # Show email instead of name

**Security audit use cases:** 'Who changed this authentication check on November 3rd?' 'Who removed the input validation from this API endpoint?' 'Who added this suspicious function?' Answers in seconds — non-repudiation through version history, the same CIA principle from Day 8 applied to code.

slides.time: 20 min · Run git blame on your own security scripts

slides.label: git stash
slides.heading: git stash: __Save Work, Switch Context__

body.text:
# Save uncommitted work to a temporary stack:
git stash
# Your working directory is now clean
# Switch to main to apply an urgent hotfix
git checkout main
git checkout -b hotfix-critical-vuln

# After fixing and merging the hotfix:
git checkout feature-dns-scanner

# Restore your stashed work:
git stash pop    # Restore latest stash (removes from stack)
git stash apply  # Restore latest stash (keeps in stack)

# Manage multiple stashes:
git stash list           # See all saved stashes
git stash show stash@{1} # See what is in stash 1
git stash drop stash@{0} # Delete a specific stash
git stash clear          # Delete ALL stashes

slides.note: **SOC analyst use:** A critical incident interrupts feature development mid-commit. Stash current work → respond to incident → pop stash when done. No lost work, no dirty commit history.
slides.time: 15 min · Practice stash during a simulated context switch

slides.label: git bisect
slides.heading: git bisect: __Find the Breaking Commit__

body.text:
# Start bisect session:
git bisect start
git bisect bad              # Current version is broken
git bisect good v1.2.0      # This version was working fine
# Git automatically checks out the midpoint commit

# At each midpoint — test the code, then tell Git:
git bisect good  # This commit is fine
git bisect bad   # This commit is broken
# Git halves the search space and checks out the next midpoint
# Repeat until Git identifies the exact culprit commit

# Git shows the exact commit:
# 'a3f2b1c is the first bad commit'
# Now examine that commit:
git show a3f2b1c

# Clean up after bisect:
git bisect reset  # Returns to original HEAD position

**Security use case:** 'When was this backdoor introduced?' `git bisect` finds the exact commit in O(log n) steps — 1000 commits requires only ~10 bisect steps. Invaluable for security audits of suspicious repositories.

slides.time: 25 min · Practice git bisect on your own repo

slides.label: What to Learn Today
slides.heading: Day 60 Checklist
steps: Run **`git log --graph --all --oneline`** on your cybersecurity-tools repo — understand the visual output
Use **`git blame`** on your port scanner — identify exactly when each section was written
Practice **`git stash`** — stash uncommitted changes, switch branches, pop stash on return
Practice **`git bisect`** — introduce a deliberate bug, make commits, find the breaking commit
Memorise **3 critical Git interview answers** — reset, revert, bisect (see Pro Tip)
slides.time: Total Day 60 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes

resources.label: 🆘 Reference
resources.name: ohshitgit.com — Fix Every Common Git Mistake

resources.label: 📖 Book
resources.name: Pro Git — Chapter 7: Git Tools (bisect, blame, stash)

slides.label: Action Plan
slides.heading: Your Hands-On __Task__ for Day 60
steps: **Introduce** a deliberate bug — remove a required import — commit it — make 3–4 more commits after
**Use** `git bisect start` → `git bisect bad` → `git bisect good v1.0` → test → good/bad → find the culprit
**Record** how many bisect steps it took — run `git bisect reset` to restore
**Memorise** these 3 interview answers: undo last commit = `git reset HEAD~1` / undo pushed commit = `git revert HEAD` / find breaking commit = `git bisect`
slides.time: Hands-On Task: 60 – 75 minutes

slides.label: Pro Tip
slides.quote: Three Git answers to memorise cold for interviews: undo last commit keeping changes = `git reset HEAD~1` | undo a pushed commit safely = `git revert HEAD` | find which commit broke things = `git bisect`.
slides.sub: When searching git history for historically committed secrets or suspicious code, use `git log -S 'api_key'` (capital S, called the 'pickaxe'). It finds every commit that added or removed the exact string — not just commits that mention it in the message. This is the forensic command for security audits of repositories.
slides.mistake: Using `git push --force` when you need to rewrite history. Force push overwrites the remote branch completely — destroying any commits your teammates pushed since you last pulled. Use `git push --force-with-lease` instead — it fails safely if anyone else has pushed since your last pull, preventing accidental destruction of teammates' work.

cover.title: GitHub Actions & __DevSecOps Pipelines__
cover.hook: Every commit triggers automated security checks. This is DevSecOps — and it starts free on GitHub.

slides.label: What are GitHub Actions?
slides.method: WHAT — GitHub Actions for Security Automation
slides.heading: GitHub Actions: __Automate Everything__

body.text: **GitHub Actions** are automated workflows triggered by Git events — a push, a pull request, a schedule, or a manual trigger. They run on GitHub's servers — zero infrastructure required. Free for public repos, 2,000 minutes per month free for private repos.
**DevSecOps principle: Shift Left.** Move security checks earlier in the development process — from 'check after deployment' to 'check at every commit.' GitHub Actions makes this free and automatic.

# Workflow files live at:
.github/workflows/security.yml

# Basic structure:
name: Security Scan
on: [push, pull_request]  # Trigger on any push or PR
jobs:
  scan:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v3
      - name: Run security scan
        run: echo 'Security check runs here'

slides.time: 20 min · docs.github.com/en/actions

slides.label: Security Use Cases
slides.method: WHAT — Security Checks You Can Automate
slides.heading: Security Automation: __Free on Every Commit__

body.text: **bandit:** Python SAST scanner — finds hardcoded passwords, use of `eval()`, SQL injection risks, weak cryptography usage. Runs in 10 seconds on any Python codebase.
**pip-audit:** Scans requirements.txt for known CVEs in Python dependencies. Alerts you when a library you use has a newly disclosed vulnerability.
**truffleHog:** Deep secret scanner — detects API keys, tokens, and credentials in code and git history. More thorough than GitHub's built-in scanning.
**semgrep:** SAST for 20+ languages — finds security bugs using pattern-based rules. Free for open source. Has pre-built rule packs for OWASP Top 10.
**trivy:** Container image vulnerability scanner — checks Docker images for known CVE-affected packages. Essential for any containerised security tool.

slides.time: 20 min · github.com/marketplace — browse free security Actions

slides.label: Your First Security Workflow
slides.method: HOW — Building a Security CI/CD Pipeline
slides.heading: Your First __Security Workflow__: bandit + pip-audit

body.text:
# .github/workflows/security.yml
name: Security Checks

on:
  push:
    branches: [ main, develop ]
  pull_request:
    branches: [ main ]

jobs:
  security-scan:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Checkout code
        uses: actions/checkout@v3
      
      - name: Set up Python
        uses: actions/setup-python@v4
        with:
          python-version: '3.11'
      
      - name: Install security tools
        run: pip install bandit pip-audit
      
      - name: Run bandit SAST
        run: bandit -r . -ll -x ./tests/  # -ll = medium+ severity
      
      - name: Run pip-audit CVE check
        run: pip-audit -r requirements.txt

slides.time: 30 min · Create this workflow in your cybersecurity-tools repo

slides.label: Scheduled Scans & GitHub Secrets
slides.heading: Scheduled Scans & __Secure__ API Key Storage

body.text:
# Schedule weekly scans (even without new commits):
on:
  schedule:
    - cron: '0 6 * * 1'  # Every Monday at 6:00 AM UTC
  push:
    branches: [ main ]

# Use GitHub Secrets for API keys (NEVER hardcode in YAML):
# GitHub repo → Settings → Secrets → Actions → New secret
# Name: VT_API_KEY
# Value: your_actual_virustotal_api_key

# Reference in workflow YAML:
steps:
  - name: Check IOCs against VirusTotal
    env:
      VT_KEY: ${{ secrets.VT_API_KEY }}
    run: python ioc_checker.py --vt-key $VT_KEY

**Scheduled scans catch new CVEs** in existing dependencies that appear AFTER your last commit. A library you installed 6 months ago may have a critical vulnerability disclosed today. Scheduled weekly scanning is the only way to know.

slides.time: 20 min · Add scheduled scanning to your security workflow

slides.label: What to Learn Today
slides.heading: Day 61 Checklist
steps: Create `.github/workflows/security.yml` with **bandit and pip-audit** steps
**Push it** to your cybersecurity-tools repo → watch the Actions tab — see the workflow run
Fix any **bandit findings** the scan reports — understand what vulnerability each finding represents
Add a **scheduled scan** using cron syntax — every Monday at 6:00 AM UTC
Store your **VirusTotal API key** in GitHub Secrets — reference it from the workflow file
slides.time: Total Day 61 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes

slides.label: Action Plan
slides.heading: Your Hands-On __Task__ for Day 61
steps: **Create** `.github/workflows/security.yml` with bandit + pip-audit steps exactly as shown
**Push** to your cybersecurity-tools repo → go to Actions tab → watch the workflow execute
**Read** the bandit output — identify any security findings — fix the highest severity ones
**Add** your VT_API_KEY to GitHub Secrets → reference it in the workflow → push and verify
slides.time: Hands-On Task: 75 – 90 minutes

slides.label: Pro Tip
slides.quote: Being able to say 'I set up a GitHub Actions CI/CD pipeline with automated security scanning' in an interview is DevSecOps experience — even from a personal project. It demonstrates shift-left security philosophy.
slides.sub: The GitHub Actions marketplace has over 3,000 pre-built Actions — many from major security vendors (Snyk, Semgrep, Checkmarx, SonarCloud). Every one of them has a free tier for public repos. Adding 2–3 security Actions to your pipeline transforms it from a basic YAML file into a professional-grade DevSecOps demonstration.
slides.mistake: Storing real secrets in workflow YAML files. Even if you think the file is private, workflow files are code — they get reviewed, forked, and versioned. Always use GitHub Secrets (Settings → Secrets → Actions) and reference them as `${{ secrets.SECRET_NAME }}` in YAML. This is the only acceptable way to use credentials in CI/CD pipelines.

cover.title: Detection-as-Code: __Sigma Rules & Git__
cover.hook: Detection rules are code. Version them, review them, deploy them — like code. This is the future of security operations.

slides.label: What is Detection-as-Code?
slides.heading: Detection-as-Code: __Rules in Git__

body.text: **Detection-as-Code** applies software engineering practices to security detection rules. Write detection rules as YAML/JSON files → store in Git → review via Pull Requests → auto-convert and deploy to SIEM. Rules are versioned, auditable, peer-reviewed, and portable.

**Sigma** is the universal detection rule format. Write a rule once in Sigma's vendor-neutral YAML format. Convert to any supported SIEM platform — Splunk SPL, Elastic KQL, Microsoft Sentinel KQL, IBM QRadar AQL — with a single command. One source of truth, infinite platform targets.

**Why Git for detection rules:** Every rule change has an author, a timestamp, and a reason. A rule that generates 10,000 false positives can be reverted in seconds. Rules can be tested on branches before deployment to production. This is how mature SOC teams operate.

slides.time: 20 min · github.com/SigmaHQ/sigma · sigmahq.io

slides.label: Sigma Rule Anatomy
slides.heading: Sigma Rule: __Every Field Explained__

body.text:
title: SSH Brute Force Detection
id: a8f2c3e1-4b7d-4f9a-b2c1-8e3d7f2a1b4c
status: stable           # stable / test / experimental
description: Detects SSH brute force from single IP
author: Your Name
date: 2026/04/23
references:
  - https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1110/001

logsource:
  product: linux
  service: auth

detection:
  keywords:
    - 'Failed password'
  filter_legit:
    - '127.0.0.1'  # Exclude localhost
  condition: keywords and not filter_legit

falsepositives:
  - Legitimate users with forgotten passwords
  - Password managers with wrong stored passwords

level: medium
tags:
  - attack.credential_access
  - attack.t1110.001

slides.time: 25 min · Analyse 5 rules from SigmaHQ/sigma — identify each field

slides.label: Sigma Rule Example
slides.heading: Real Rule: __PowerShell Encoded Command__ (T1059.001)

body.text:
title: PowerShell Encoded Command Execution
id: b9d3c4f2-5e8a-4c1b-a3d2-9f4e8c3b2a5d
status: stable
description: Detects PowerShell execution with encoded commands.
  Attackers use -EncodedCommand to obfuscate malicious scripts.
author: Security Team
tags:
  - attack.execution
  - attack.t1059.001

logsource:
  category: process_creation
  product: windows

detection:
  selection:
    Image|endswith: '\powershell.exe'
    CommandLine|contains:
      - '-EncodedCommand'
      - '-enc '
      - '-ec '
  condition: selection

falsepositives:
  - Legitimate administrative scripts using encoding
  - Software installation scripts

level: high
fields:
  - CommandLine
  - ParentImage
  - User

slides.time: 20 min · Write this rule and convert it using uncoder.io

slides.label: Converting with sigma-cli
slides.heading: sigma-cli: __One Rule, Every Platform__

body.text:
# Install sigma-cli:
pip install sigma-cli

# List available backends (target platforms):
sigma list backends

# Convert Sigma rule to different platforms:
sigma convert -t splunk rules/windows/powershell_encoded.yml
# Output: Splunk SPL query ready to paste

sigma convert -t elasticsearch rules/windows/powershell_encoded.yml
# Output: Elasticsearch query in JSON

sigma convert -t sentinel rules/windows/powershell_encoded.yml
# Output: Microsoft Sentinel KQL

**Free online alternative:** uncoder.io converts Sigma rules in the browser — no installation needed. Paste your YAML, select target platform, get the converted query instantly. Perfect for exploring conversion output without CLI setup.

**3,000+ free community rules** in the SigmaHQ GitHub repo — covering Windows, Linux, cloud, network, web, and application log sources. Clone the repo and you have a detection library that enterprise SOC teams pay for.

slides.time: 20 min · Convert your SSH brute-force rule to 3 different platforms

Pick **3 rules** mapped to ATT&CK techniques from earlier weeks — read every field carefully
**Convert** each rule using uncoder.io — to Elasticsearch, Splunk, and Sentinel formats
**Write your own** SSH brute-force rule — all required fields, proper ATT&CK tags
**Store** your rule in your GitHub repo in a `sigma-rules/` directory — version control your detections

resources.label: 🔄 Converter
resources.name: uncoder.io — Free Online Sigma Rule Converter

**Pick** 3 rules you recognise from earlier phases — document what each detects and what log source

slides.time: Hands-On Task: 75 – 90 minutes

slides.label: Pro Tip
slides.quote: Detection Engineering is one of the fastest-growing, highest-paying security specialisations — $100–140k entry level at top companies. It combines SIEM + Sigma/YARA + Git + MITRE ATT&CK. Contributing even one PR to the SigmaHQ repo gives you public, verifiable proof of detection engineering skill.
slides.sub: The SigmaHQ repository accepts community contributions. If you write a quality detection rule that is not already covered in the repo — for a new malware technique, a novel attack pattern, or a cloud service not yet represented — submit a PR. A merged PR to SigmaHQ on your GitHub profile is a credential that no certification replicates.
slides.mistake: Deploying Sigma rules to production SIEM without testing against real log samples first. An untested rule with a broad pattern can fire thousands of false positive alerts — burying legitimate detections and burning out the SOC team investigating them. Always test against a representative sample of your logs before enabling in production.

cover.title: Git Week __Recap__ & Portfolio Push
cover.hook: Git week complete. 63 days in. Your GitHub is now your loudest professional statement. Let us finalise it.

slides.label: Git Week Full Recap
slides.heading: 7 Days of Git: __Everything You Learned__

body.text: **Day 57:** Git init, add, commit, push — the 4 core commands. First-time setup. GitHub account and SSH keys. First portfolio repo live.
**Day 58:** Branching (`checkout -b`), merging, conflict resolution. Full Pull Request workflow on GitHub. Git Flow branching strategy.
**Day 59:** GitHub profile optimisation, professional README structure, secret scanning, push protection, pre-commit hooks, .gitignore essentials.
**Day 60:** git log (audit trail), git blame (accountability), git stash (context switching), git bisect (binary search for breaking commits).
**Day 61:** GitHub Actions CI/CD — bandit SAST, pip-audit dependency scanning, scheduled weekly scans, GitHub Secrets for API keys.
**Day 62:** Detection-as-Code with Sigma — rule anatomy, PowerShell detection example, sigma-cli conversion to Splunk/Elasticsearch/Sentinel, SigmaHQ community repo.

slides.time: 30 min · Review all 7 days of notes — git-scm.com/book for lifetime reference

slides.label: Portfolio Checklist
slides.method: WHAT — Your GitHub Must Show This
slides.heading: Portfolio Checklist: __Audit Your GitHub__

body.text: ✅ **6 repos pinned** on your profile — covering port scanner, banner grabber, OSINT tool, IOC enricher, log parser, Sigma rules
✅ **Every repo has a README** with tool description, installation, usage example, and ethical use disclaimer
✅ **Every Python repo has requirements.txt** — generated with `pip freeze > requirements.txt`
✅ **.gitignore on every repo** — no secrets, no venv, no __pycache__
✅ **GitHub Actions security pipeline** on at least one repo — bandit + pip-audit running on every push
✅ **Profile README** — skills, current 90-day progress, top 3 projects, LinkedIn link
✅ **Consistent commit history** — green squares spanning 63+ days — visible proof of dedication

slides.time: 30 min · Audit every repo against this checklist right now

Your Python log parsing skills (Phase 4) + Linux administration (Phase 3) + Git workflow (this week) + traffic analysis (Phase 5) all combine into building a complete, professional SIEM with Elasticsearch, Logstash, and Kibana.

**ELK is the most transformative week of the entire 90-day series.** You will build a real-time security monitoring platform from scratch — ingesting your own system logs, visualising attacks in Kibana dashboards, and alerting on threats. This is exactly what enterprise SOC teams use.

slides.label: Pro Tip
slides.quote: A GitHub profile with 6+ documented security projects, a professional README, active commit history, and a GitHub Actions pipeline is worth more in technical hiring than most entry-level certifications.
slides.sub: Security engineering managers at top-tier companies explicitly say this: a strong GitHub portfolio with demonstrated, working tools beats a list of certifications from candidates who cannot write a working script. The reason is simple — certifications test knowledge, GitHub shows capability. Both are needed, but capability is rarer and more immediately valuable.
slides.mistake: Quantity over quality in your GitHub portfolio. Three exceptional, fully documented, working tools beat twenty empty repos with no README every single time. Employers click through. They open the code. They run the README instructions. A broken installation process, a missing requirements.txt, or an empty README immediately signals that the tool was built for show, not for use. Build fewer things, build them properly.
